title: The Wife

jokes.text: The dry cleaning relationship bit — 'He has it as much as you do. You ought to go shopping with him.'
jokes.setup: Jerry frames the dry cleaner relationship as a back-and-forth exchange where the cleaner holds the garment as often as the owner
jokes.punchline: The logical but absurd conclusion: the cleaner should go shopping with you since it's practically half his shirt
jokes.explanation: Solid observational stand-up bit with a logical escalation to an absurd conclusion. The 'half his shirt' reframe is clever, and the shopping invitation punchline follows naturally. Competent Seinfeld stand-up but not his sharpest material.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how when you take something to the dry cleaners, it's like you're entering into a relationship with them? You're like, 'I'm trusting you with my clothes.' And they're like, 'Yeah, we got it.' But you never really know if they're gonna take care of it. It could come back shrunk, it could come back with a stain, it could come back a completely different color. You're basically in a committed relationship with the dry cleaners. You're sharing ownership of your clothes!

jokes.text: 'The only warning label people respect is dry-clean only.'
jokes.setup: People ignore life-threatening warnings like speed limits and cigarette cancer warnings
jokes.punchline: But they are scrupulously obedient about dry-clean only labels — a trivial clothing instruction
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely exceptional stand-up writing. The insight is sharp, the contrast (death vs. laundry instructions) is perfectly calibrated, and the economy is masterful — one sentence does all the work. The follow-up voices ('Don't put that in the wash. Are you crazy?') perfectly nail the absurd priority inversion. Earns the 8+ because it's a genuinely original, tight observation that still resonates.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now you're shopping with your dry cleaner? You're at the Gap together, you're picking out shirts?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's like, 'No, that color makes you look washed out.' You're like, 'You know what? You're right. You ARE my dry cleaner.'

jokes.text: 'Good morning.' / 'Good morning.' / 'How did you sleep?' / 'You're on the couch tonight.'
jokes.setup: Standard morning pleasantries exchange — 'Good morning,' 'How did you sleep?' suggests warm domestic bliss
jokes.punchline: The response to 'How did you sleep?' is not an answer but a preemptive punishment: 'You're on the couch tonight' — the fight has already begun
jokes.explanation: Tight misdirection: the mundane question is completely sidestepped by an already-active grievance. Good economy and solid structural subversion of the expected domestic scene. Earns its score through the sheer compression of the joke — the marriage dynamic is established in one line.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what warning label people actually respect? Dry clean only. That's it. That's the one. You could have a label that says 'Warning: this garment will kill you,' people are like, 'Eh, I'll take my chances.' But dry clean only? Nobody touches it. You could leave that shirt on a bench in the middle of the ghetto, it would be there for three years. 'Don't worry, it says dry clean only.'

jokes.text: 'I was sleeping with one cheek off the bed.'
jokes.setup: Meryl accuses Jerry of being all over her side of the bed
jokes.punchline: Jerry's defense — that he was hanging off the edge with one cheek off the bed — is so extreme it actually validates the accusation while meaning to refute it
jokes.explanation: The self-defeating defense is a reliable comedy structure. The specificity of 'one cheek' is what saves it from being generic. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: People are very reverent about clothing care instructions. They treat them like they're from God. 'Dry clean only.' Like if you put it in the washing machine, the sweater's gonna call your house and go, 'What did you do?! I was dry clean only!'

jokes.text: 'By the way, you're falling way behind on the 'I love you's.' / 'No, no, 12-8.' / 'No, it's 15-8.'
jokes.setup: A couple arguing about chores or relationship fairness
jokes.punchline: They are literally scorekeeping 'I love you's' like a sports match, with competing tallies
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original and extremely well-structured. Competitive scorekeeping of 'I love you's' is a brilliant absurdist inversion of romantic sentiment — love expressed as a game with disputed stats. The competing tallies (12-8 vs 15-8) add a perfect layer. This earns 8+ because it's a concept you don't forget and it efficiently encapsulates the episode's central theme (fake marriage reveals real relationship dynamics).

jokes.text: 'I know I can't beat you. I'm trying to stay competitive.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is losing the 'I love you' count badly
jokes.punchline: Rather than trying to win, he's just trying not to be blown out — treating romantic affirmations like a sports team in a losing season
jokes.explanation: A great callback that extends joke #5's absurdist premise. The sports-metaphor language applied to love ('stay competitive') is perfectly delivered and deeply in character for Jerry's detached, analytical persona. Good economy and strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're taking up the whole bed!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I need my space when I sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your space? I'm hanging off the edge with one cheek on the mattress!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Then get a bigger bed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have a bigger bed. You somehow expand to fill it. You're like a gas.

jokes.text: 'I think I'll try a sports jacket and scarf thing. You know, like an unemployed actor.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is deciding what to wear
jokes.punchline: The fashion aesthetic he describes has a very specific, deflating social category attached to it
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational joke with great economy. 'Unemployed actor' is a perfectly chosen descriptor — specific enough to be a real social type, deflating enough to be funny. Classic Seinfeld character-voice humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I love you too.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's one-one.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? That's two-one, my way.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You can't count the first one. That was a throwaway 'I love you.'

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A throwaway? There's no such thing as a throwaway 'I love you.' All 'I love yous' count.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not true. That was clearly a throwaway.

jokes.text: 'Cashmere?' / 'No, Gore-Tex.'
jokes.setup: Meryl feels the jacket and asks if it's cashmere, suggesting luxury
jokes.punchline: It's Gore-Tex — a purely functional, decidedly un-luxurious synthetic material, delivered without embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Clean, tight misdirection. The setup primes for luxury, the punchline delivers its polar opposite. Two words do all the work. Solid economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I realized? We keep score in relationships like it's a sport. She says 'I love you,' I say 'I love you too.' But inside I'm thinking, 'That's one for her.'

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm saying, every romantic gesture is a point. Who's ahead? Who's winning? It's like tennis, but with feelings.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what happens when it's tied?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Deuce. It's always deuce.

jokes.text: 'We can't both get pancakes. It's embarrassing. That's one step from the couples that dress alike.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Meryl both want pancakes for breakfast
jokes.punchline: Ordering the same food as your partner is equated with the most embarrassing couples' behavior — matching outfits — on a bizarre social hierarchy
jokes.explanation: A very strong Seinfeld-ian observational joke about the unspoken social codes of coupledom. The leap from 'same food order' to 'couples that dress alike' is perfectly calibrated absurdism — it's ridiculous but somehow feels true. Jerry's horror at pancake-matching is pitch-perfect character work. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm wearing sweatpants. You know what that tells the world? 'I've given up.' I'm basically dressed like an unemployed actor.

jokes.text: 'I'll get the short stack.' / 'That's why I love you.'
jokes.setup: Jerry just gave a whole speech about pancake-ordering embarrassment
jokes.punchline: Meryl's 'I love you' is prompted not by romance but by Jerry's pancake sacrifice — and it feeds directly into the scorekeeping gag
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: the mundane trigger for 'I love you' (pancake compromise) is funny in itself, and it's a callback to the scorekeeping bit, meaning the audience knows the count just ticked up. Good layering.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What is this fabric? It's so expensive!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's Gore-Tex.

jokes.text: Jerry silently counting: '15-9.' (reaction beat after Meryl says 'That's why I love you')
jokes.setup: Meryl says 'I love you' in response to the pancake compromise
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately updates the running tally — '15-9' — shown or implied as a mental scoreboard
jokes.explanation: The running gag paying off in a beat: Jerry mechanically tracking the score reinforces the absurdism. The economy is near-perfect — one number does everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what bothers me? When couples dress alike. The matching outfits, the coordinated colors...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like they're one person. They lose their identity. They become a unit.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? We're ordering the same breakfast. We're becoming one of those couples.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: We're not dressing alike.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's the same principle. We're matching. Eggs and toast, eggs and toast. Next thing you know, we'll be finishing each other's sentences.

jokes.text: 'Is that my maple syrup?' / 'Yeah.' / 'You bring your own syrup?' / 'You got to. You got a lot to learn about pancakes.'
jokes.setup: Kramer spots his own maple syrup being used at Jerry's place — he apparently left it there
jokes.punchline: Kramer has a proprietary syrup he brings to restaurants and delivers the judgment 'You got a lot to learn about pancakes' with complete authority
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer joke — he operates by rules invisible to everyone else, delivered with absolute confidence. 'You got a lot to learn about pancakes' is a great line because it's simultaneously condescending and absurd. Strong character integration: this joke only works with Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know what? Fine. Pancakes. I'll eat pancakes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great. That moves you up to a 7.

jokes.text: Dry cleaner gives 25% discount after sentimental moment, and Meryl immediately claims it: 'I guess I get it too, because I'm his wife.'
jokes.setup: A touching moment where the dry cleaner finds a locket of his dead wife and is moved to give a discount out of gratitude
jokes.punchline: Meryl immediately and shamelessly leverages the sentimental moment for financial gain
jokes.explanation: The pivot from genuine sentiment to shameless opportunism in one breath is well-timed. Good situational irony — the generosity is exploited immediately. This moment also plants the seed for the entire episode's plot engine.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's syrup.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Where did you get it?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I made it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You made syrup?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah. It's all about the temperature and the timing. You gotta know when to pull it off the heat.

jokes.text: 'You never met my wife, Meryl. Meryl Seinfeld.'
jokes.setup: Jerry introduces Meryl as his wife to the dry cleaner to get the discount
jokes.punchline: He says 'Meryl Seinfeld' with total confidence despite this being a completely fake marriage constructed for dry cleaning discounts
jokes.explanation: The confidence of the lie is the joke — and it sets up the entire plot. Functional and well-delivered but not exceptional writing on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look at this picture I found in my locket.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: That's my son! I've been looking for him for thirty years!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your son? He's been dead for thirty years.

jokes.text: 'You might regret that. The money my wife spends on clothes—' / 'I'm taking him to the cleaners.' / 'See her sense of humor?'
jokes.setup: Jerry warns the dry cleaner about his 'wife's' spending habits
jokes.punchline: Meryl delivers the perfectly apt pun 'I'm taking him to the cleaners' — in an actual dry cleaning establishment — and Jerry proudly presents it as evidence of her charms
jokes.explanation: The pun lands because of perfect situational context — saying 'I'm taking him to the cleaners' inside a dry cleaners is a rare case of a pun being genuinely clever rather than cheap. Jerry's proud presentation of it adds a warm character beat. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: You find your wife's locket, I give you 10% off your next cleaning.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: That's what I can do.

jokes.text: 'It was fun being single. But when you meet a woman like this, you don't walk to get married. You run.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is performing the role of besotted husband for the dry cleaner
jokes.punchline: The enthusiastic declaration of marriage love is completely hollow — they're pretending — making Jerry's romantic speech deeply ironic
jokes.explanation: The irony works contextually — we know this is fake — but the line itself isn't particularly original. Functional dramatic irony, competently executed.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, you get a discount? What discount?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, the guy said if I was married, I'd get a discount on the dry cleaning.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Married? You're not married.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but he just assumed.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, what if I said I was your wife? Would I get the discount?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't think that's how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why not? I could be your wife. We could split the savings. That's what married people do.

jokes.text: 'Today was the first day I've worked out since the Central Park mini-marathon.' / 'You ran the mini-marathon?' / 'No, but I exercised that day.'
jokes.setup: Elaine mentions the mini-marathon as a reference point for when she last worked out, implying athletic participation
jokes.punchline: She didn't run it — she just happened to exercise on that day. The marathon was irrelevant to her fitness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The setup perfectly implies athletic achievement; the punchline is a masterwork of deflation. 'No, but I exercised that day' is an absurdly precise non-answer that redefines the sentence that preceded it. Strong structure, great economy, very quotable. Earns 8+ because the punchline construction is genuinely impressive — it takes a split second to land, then hits hard.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'd like you to meet my wife, Newtia.

dialogue.speaker: Other
dialogue.line: Newtia? That's an unusual name.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's a combination. My name is Jerry, her name is Olivia. Jerry... Olivia... Newtia.

jokes.text: 'As an airline pilot, you're one of the few people who can say that and mean it.' (after Greg says 'I gotta take off')
jokes.setup: Greg, an airline pilot, says 'I gotta take off' as a casual goodbye
jokes.punchline: Elaine notes the double meaning — 'take off' is literal for a pilot
jokes.explanation: A competent pun joke. The observation is correct but puns about pilots saying 'take off' aren't especially original. Elaine's delivery saves it slightly — she says it earnestly rather than as a groaner.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My wife, she spends money like it's going out of style. I'm telling you, our credit card bills are insane.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, maybe she's just trying to keep up with inflation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's funny. That's actually funny.

jokes.text: Kramer attempting to get maple syrup from another diner at Jerry's table — wordlessly trying to borrow it
jokes.setup: Kramer's prized maple syrup has been brought to a restaurant (or apartment breakfast) and another person has it
jokes.punchline: Kramer's fumbling attempt to reclaim or share his syrup with a stranger plays as awkward physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy extending the syrup bit. Works as character comedy for Kramer but is relatively thin on its own without stronger visual payoff described in the transcript.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Marriage is great! It's wonderful! You know, people say marriage is hard work, but they're wrong. It's the best thing that ever happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, think about it - you get to spend every single day with your best friend. Every meal together, every night, every morning. What could be better than that?

jokes.text: 'My wife and I will have a little more coffee.' / 'And the check for my husband.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Meryl are play-acting as husband and wife at breakfast
jokes.punchline: They're both hamming up the 'husband/wife' language to a waiter, reveling in the performance of domesticity they've constructed for a dry cleaning discount
jokes.explanation: Enjoyable irony — both characters leaning into 'my husband/my wife' with genuine delight. Sets up Jerry's later monologue about loving saying 'my wife.' Competent situational irony.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So you ran the marathon?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't exactly run it. I walked most of it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You walked the marathon?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Yeah, I trained for months. I ran every single day.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Every single day? For months?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Well, actually just that one day. The day of the marathon.

jokes.text: 'To my beautiful wife.' / 'To my adoring husband.' / 'Adoring? What about handsome?' / 'I like adoring.' / 'Yeah, sure. Adoring's good for you. What's it do for me?'
jokes.setup: They toast to each other with married-couple superlatives
jokes.punchline: Jerry rejects 'adoring' as a compliment because it benefits her emotionally but does nothing for his vanity — 'What's it do for me?'
jokes.explanation: Neat character joke about Jerry's vanity and the transactional nature of compliments. 'What's it do for me?' is a strong punchline — it captures Jerry's personality efficiently. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Take off? What do you mean, take off?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I'm a pilot. I have to take off.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're taking off your clothes right here?

jokes.text: Manager confronts Kramer about bringing outside syrup to the restaurant: 'We don't allow any outside syrups, jams, or condiments in the restaurant... and if I catch you in here with that again, I will confiscate it.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has been brazenly bringing his own maple syrup to a restaurant
jokes.punchline: The restaurant has a formal policy against outside condiments, and Kramer faces actual bureaucratic consequences — confiscation — for his syrup eccentricity
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the syrup bit to a formal conflict with authority is classic Seinfeld structure — a trivial obsession colliding with institutional bureaucracy. Strong character integration for Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What time is it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: 2:47? How do you know it's exactly 2:47?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I'm a pilot. We have to be precise about time.

jokes.text: 'Well, I told my wife not to bring it.' (Kramer deflecting blame onto the absent Meryl)
jokes.setup: Kramer is caught with his contraband syrup
jokes.punchline: Rather than own it, he immediately blames a wife he doesn't have — and pins it on the character who has been playing 'Jerry's wife'
jokes.explanation: Masterful joke because it simultaneously extends two running gags (the syrup conflict and the fake marriage) while showcasing Kramer's brazen, consequence-free personality. The convergence of storylines in one throwaway line is great Seinfeld writing. Earns 8+ for how efficiently it layers multiple comedic threads.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Can I have some syrup?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: You want to try my special syrup?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, I just want syrup.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Nobody just wants syrup. They want to try my special syrup.

jokes.text: 'Really? Twenty-five percent off? Do I get that too?' / 'No. Just Meryl.' / 'Why does she get it?' / 'Because she's my wife.'
jokes.setup: Kramer hears about the dry cleaning discount
jokes.punchline: Kramer immediately wants the same deal, and the 'because she's my wife' reasoning is absurd since the marriage is fake — but treated as legitimate within the scene
jokes.explanation: Competent running gag beat. Kramer's jealousy and Jerry's po-faced 'because she's my wife' creates good irony given the fake marriage context. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's my wife.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's my husband.

jokes.text: Jerry's monologue: 'I love saying my wife. Once I started saying it, I can't stop. My wife this, my wife that. It's an amazing way to begin a sentence. My wife has an inner ear infection.'
jokes.setup: Jerry explains he's enjoying playing married
jokes.punchline: The climax of this love for 'my wife' as a sentence opener is the aggressively mundane example: 'My wife has an inner ear infection' — a sentence nobody says proudly
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The buildup about loving 'my wife' as a sentence prefix pays off with an exquisitely anti-climactic example — inner ear infection is perfectly chosen for its banality. The joke understands that the punchline has to be as mundane as possible to work. Strong character integration (Jerry's analytical love of language), great structure, and highly quotable. This is Seinfeld at its observational best.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is this? You're toasting me with 'adoring'? I wanted 'handsome.' 'Adoring' is what you say about a cocker spaniel.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, you are adoring.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't want to be adoring! I want to be handsome, mysterious, intriguing. Adoring is for golden retrievers and infants.

jokes.text: Meryl asks Jerry to take her quilt to the cleaners to use the discount — the fake marriage becoming a domestic obligation
jokes.setup: Jerry has been bragging about the joys of saying 'my wife'
jokes.punchline: Immediately, the 'wife' starts making spousal demands — the fantasy of marriage collides with the banal reality
jokes.explanation: Good structural irony — Jerry's romantic speech about loving marriage is immediately undercut by marital obligation. Classic Seinfeld deflation of sentiment.

dialogue.speaker: Restaurant manager
dialogue.line: You can't bring outside syrup in here. That's contraband. I'm going to have to confiscate that.

jokes.text: Elaine proposes: 'What do you say, if neither of us is married in 10 years, we get hitched?' / 'Let's make it 50.' / 'We're engaged.'
jokes.setup: Elaine makes the classic 'backup spouse' pact with Jerry
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately pushes it to 50 years — then Elaine, ignoring his distancing, declares them engaged
jokes.explanation: Works on the irony of Jerry already being 'fake married' while making a 'backup' pact with Elaine. The '50 years' counter and Elaine's blunt 'we're engaged' declaration are good economy jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Jerry, we need to talk about the syrup incident.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that? That was my wife. She did that. I had nothing to do with it.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Your wife?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, completely out of control. I apologize on her behalf.

jokes.text: Elaine's detailed anatomical breakdown of the open-lip kiss: 'His upper lip landed flush on my upper lip, but his lower lip landed well below my rim.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is trying to decode whether a kiss was a romantic signal
jokes.punchline: The clinical, engineering-level precision of her lip-position analysis is absurdly detailed for what is essentially a social interaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy and observational joke. The geometric precision applied to something as casual and emotional as a hello kiss is perfectly Elaine — she's treating it like a technical report. 'Well below my rim' is a great specific detail. Strong character integration and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What discount?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, some kind of senior citizen discount or something.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Senior citizen? I'm not a senior citizen!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you have to be a certain age.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: How much is the discount?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I think it's like ten percent.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Ten percent?! I want that discount!

jokes.text: 'Moisture?' / 'Yeah. Definite moisture.' / 'That's an open-lip kiss, all right.'
jokes.setup: Elaine finishes her technical kiss analysis
jokes.punchline: Jerry's clinical confirmation — 'definite moisture' as the diagnostic criterion — treats the romantic moment like a forensic analysis
jokes.explanation: The follow-through on the clinical kiss analysis. 'Definite moisture' as the diagnostic criterion for an open-lip kiss is brilliantly specific and gross in the best way. Jerry's deadpan confirmation 'That's an open-lip kiss, all right' treats this like medical testimony. Earns 8+ for the specific word choice ('moisture') and the perfect deadpan structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's great about marriage? You get to wake up every morning and know exactly what's going to happen. There's no mystery, no surprise, no danger. It's like watching a rerun of a show you've already seen.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But dating? Dating is the real thing. Dating is the adventure. And yet here I am, more excited about this fake marriage scenario than I ever was about any actual date.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The irony is just killing me.

jokes.text: George's confession: 'I was in the locker room showering, and I... I had to go, so—' / 'Here we go.'
jokes.setup: George has been mysteriously absent; he's finally explaining what happened with growing reluctance
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'Here we go' reveals he already knows where this is heading — George has a history, the audience has been prepped for a confession
jokes.explanation: The 'Here we go' is doing enormous work — it implies this isn't George's first offense and that Jerry has heard confessions like this before. Strong character integration for both characters. The dread-reveal structure is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have this thing now where I just want to say 'my wife.' Like, 'My wife, she does this thing...' or 'My wife hates when I...' I don't even have a wife! But I love saying it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So yesterday I'm at the coffee shop, and some guy next to me sneezes. And I turn to him and I go, 'My wife, she had an inner ear infection once.' And he just stares at me.

jokes.text: George urinated in the shower at the health club
jokes.setup: George has been building up to a shameful confession about what happened at the gym
jokes.punchline: He peed in the shower and someone saw him — a fundamentally disgusting but universally recognizable taboo
jokes.explanation: The shower urination reveal works because it's both relatable (the act itself) and shameful (the witness). Strong George character integration — this is exactly the kind of thing George would do and get caught doing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Jerry, I need you to take my quilt to the cleaners.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why do I have to take your quilt to the cleaners?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Because you're my fake husband.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not your fake husband.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yes you are. You're everyone's fake husband.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I am not everyone's fake husband!

jokes.text: 'You went... in the shower?' / 'Yeah, so what? I'm not the only one.' / 'Do you go in the shower?' / 'No, never.' / 'Do you?' / 'I take baths.'
jokes.setup: George defends his shower urination by claiming others do it too
jokes.punchline: Quick poll of the group: Jerry refuses, Elaine sidesteps with 'I take baths' — a response that neither confirms nor denies but cleverly distances
jokes.explanation: Elaine's 'I take baths' is the star of this exchange — it's the perfect evasion, simultaneously prudish and suspicious. The rapid escalation from George's claim to the group poll to Elaine's deflection is structurally excellent. 'I take baths' is highly quotable and earns the 8+ for structure and Elaine's perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So if I ever propose to you, and you say no, I have a backup plan.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What backup plan?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I ask you again in six months.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, I have a counteroffer. You propose to me, I'll say yes, we'll be engaged for 50 years, and then we'll get married.

jokes.text: 'What was I supposed to do? Get out of the shower, put on my bathrobe, go to the other end, come all the way back?' / 'Did you ever hear of holding it in?' / 'Oh, no. That's very bad for the kidneys.' / 'How do you know?' / 'Medical journals.'
jokes.setup: George justifies his shower urination with increasingly elaborate rationales
jokes.punchline: When Jerry suggests holding it, George cites 'medical journals' to support his kidney theory — George invoking medical authority for an absurd position
jokes.explanation: George citing 'medical journals' for his shower-peeing rationalization is a perfect character joke. The authoritative citation for the most convenient possible conclusion is peak George Costanza — self-serving logic dressed up as intellectual rigor. 'Medical journals' is a genuinely great two-word punchline. Earns high scores for character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's a kiss where you kind of bite their lip a little bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a kiss, that's a snack.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, it's very sensual. You bite, then you kiss, then you bite again.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not sensual, that's a chew toy.

jokes.text: 'Do the medical journals mention anything about standing in a pool of someone else's urine?'
jokes.setup: George has just invoked medical journals to justify not holding it in
jokes.punchline: Jerry's perfectly dry counter-citation turns the medical-authority framing against George by pointing out the consequence for the other shower-user
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode. Jerry's counter uses George's own 'medical journals' framing against him to devastatingly comic effect. The specificity ('pool of someone else's urine') is perfectly revolting and funny. The structure is flawless: setup establishes the pseudo-medical frame, punchline weaponizes it. Highly quotable, great economy, and an irreversible sentence — once you hear it you can't unhear it. Near-legendary line.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It was like a technical kiss. No lips, no nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Was there any moisture?

jokes.text: Meryl drops off more dry cleaning at Jerry's apartment, revealing she's been treating the fake-marriage discount as a real ongoing benefit
jokes.setup: The fake marriage was supposed to be a one-time discount
jokes.punchline: Meryl is now routing all her dry cleaning through Jerry's 'husband' account — the scheme has created real domestic obligations
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of the fake-marriage plot. The comedy is structural — the consequences of a trivial scheme becoming real — but the beat itself isn't especially quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you're going to categorize every kiss? Like it's some kind of scientific study?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, yes. The kiss on the cheek, that's friendly. The kiss on the lips, that's romantic. The kiss on the forehead, that's paternal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What about the kiss on the neck?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's... that's passionate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And you're keeping notes?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Charts. I have charts.

jokes.text: Uncle Leo finds out Jerry got 'married' and wasn't invited: 'I wasn't invited? Nobody sends me an invitation?'
jokes.setup: Uncle Leo overhears/learns Jerry got married
jokes.punchline: Leo's wounded outrage at not being invited escalates rapidly — his sense of familial grievance is disproportionate to the situation (especially since there was no real wedding)
jokes.explanation: Classic Uncle Leo behavior — every slight is a catastrophe. 'I wasn't invited? Nobody sends me an invitation?' is good character comedy in his established mode. Strong character integration but not a particularly original joke for Leo.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're not interested in what I have to say?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, I respect that you're ignoring me.

jokes.text: 'Are you ashamed of your uncle? Do I embarrass you?' / 'No, no, it was a small ceremony.' / 'Haven't I always been the good uncle?'
jokes.setup: Jerry tries to manage Uncle Leo's hurt feelings
jokes.punchline: Leo escalates to existential questions about their relationship ('Do I embarrass you?') and immediately pivots to self-promotion ('Haven't I always been the good uncle?')
jokes.explanation: The self-promotional 'haven't I always been the good uncle?' is a great Leo beat — he weaponizes his goodness as leverage. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So there I am, in the middle of this conversation...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh no. Where is this going?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And I realize I have to sneeze.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Just sneeze, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't! There's a woman right there. So I'm holding it in, and I'm turning red, and my eyes are watering...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: This better not be what I think it is.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I finally let it out, and—

jokes.text: 'Who told you when you went to school that you print well?' / 'You did.' / 'He had a beautiful penmanship.' / 'I remember your V. It was like a perfect triangle.'
jokes.setup: Uncle Leo is cataloguing his credentials as the good uncle
jokes.punchline: His proudest example of being a good uncle is... telling young Jerry that he printed well. And he has a specific memory of the letter V. The absurd specificity deflates the grandeur of his argument
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent. Uncle Leo's 'good uncle' credentials peak at: complimenting childhood penmanship. The V as a 'perfect triangle' is a beautifully absurd specific detail. This is deep character writing — Leo's entire sense of self-worth is built on trivial encouragements he gave decades ago. Earns 8+ for originality, character integration, and the specific detail of the V. A memorable Leo moment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm in the shower, I'm washing, and suddenly I feel this... urge. And I think to myself, 'No, George, not now. You're in the shower.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What did you do?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean what did I do? I did it. I'm in the shower!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: In the shower?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It goes right down the drain!

jokes.text: 'Oh, there's my bus.' / 'Uncle Leo. Uncle—' (Leo runs for bus, abandoning conversation)
jokes.setup: Uncle Leo has been in the middle of an emotionally intense grievance monologue
jokes.punchline: He immediately abandons it to chase his bus — the bus is more important than the marriage betrayal
jokes.explanation: Great structural joke — the emotional intensity of the scene is punctured by the mundane pragmatism of catching public transit. The abrupt abandonment is perfectly Leo. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You eat your cereal with orange juice?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not? It's juice, it's liquid. A lot of people do it.

jokes.text: 'You know where I can get some good olives?' / 'I can find out.' / 'Would you?' / 'Sure.' / 'Oh, a project. That's a definite signal.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is trying to read signals from Greg (the pilot)
jokes.punchline: His request for olive recommendations, which she interprets as a 'project' and therefore a 'definite signal' of romantic interest, is hilariously overread
jokes.explanation: Elaine's dating-signal paranoia is perfectly captured — she's turned a casual condiment question into evidence of romantic investment. Good character comedy and economy. 'A project. That's a definite signal.' is a quotable non-sequitur.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm asking you, do you take showers?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you ever urinate in the shower?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not gonna lie to you, it's never crossed my mind.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You never thought about it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's one of the great mysteries of life, I don't know anybody that does it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I take baths.

jokes.text: 'By the way, you look really great in that leotard.' / 'Thanks.' (Elaine's internal monologue: 'That's no signal. Who wouldn't like me in this leotard? I look amazing in this leotard.')
jokes.setup: Elaine is cataloguing all of Greg's signals
jokes.punchline: A genuine compliment — the clearest signal so far — is DISMISSED by Elaine because she figures anyone would say that. She's over-thinking to the point of rejecting real evidence
jokes.explanation: The irony here is perfect: Elaine reads olive procurement as a signal but dismisses a direct compliment. Her unfiltered vanity ('I look amazing in this leotard') is funny in itself, and the inversion of what counts as a signal is a well-constructed joke. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not sick! I just have a very strict code. I don't urinate in the shower. It's unsanitary.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, everyone urinates in the shower.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Not me. I have a system. I wake up, I go to the bathroom, I urinate, then I shower. It's called order.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What if you're already in the shower?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I get out. I go to the bathroom. I come back in. It's a process, but it works for me.

jokes.text: 'I think I had a dream about you last night.' (Greg to Elaine) / Elaine's internal: 'Okay, he open-lips me, he dreams about me, we have an olive project, that's it. I'm asking this guy out.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has been accumulating romantic 'signals' from Greg
jokes.punchline: The dream revelation pushes Elaine to her decision threshold — her internal checklist of signals is finally complete, and she acts
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the signal-tracking running gag. The internal checklist summarizing all previous signals is a neat structural callback, though the joke itself is more plot than pure comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why don't you just tell her the truth?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The truth? Jerry, I'm reading about a condition in the American Journal of Medicine. It says here that certain men have a psychological block that prevents them from... you know.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're making that up.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not making it up! It's right here. 'Fear of inadequacy syndrome.' Very common. I could have that.

jokes.text: "I think I had a dream about you last night." (Elaine internal) "Okay, he open-lips me, he dreams about me, we have an olive project — that's it. I'm asking this guy out."
jokes.setup: Greg casually mentions a dream — Elaine has been accumulating 'signals'
jokes.punchline: The three-item inventory ('open lips, dreams, olive project') crosses her threshold — she's decided
jokes.explanation: The comedic payoff of Elaine's signal-cataloging. The absurdity of 'olive project' being one of three decisive romantic criteria is hilarious. Well-constructed escalation to a decision.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is, if someone wrote in a medical journal that they cured cancer with a ham sandwich, that would be legitimate medical advice?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was in a journal!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A journal is just a book, George. Anyone can write anything in a book. I could write 'George Costanza cured my erectile dysfunction' in a journal, that doesn't make it true!

jokes.text: Elaine is about to ask Greg out, offers him water — he wipes the top of the bottle
jokes.setup: Elaine has finally decided to ask Greg out and offers him water as an opening
jokes.punchline: He wipes the top of the water bottle before drinking — a gesture Elaine will later interpret as a devastating signal of non-interest
jokes.explanation: The visual gag plants the seed for the 'bottle-wipe' theory later. On first viewing it seems neutral; retrospectively it's the hinge of the Elaine subplot. Good structural planting.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I was at the coffee shop, but then I had to go to the dry cleaners, and then I stopped by the bank to deposit a check, and then I realized I was hungry so I went to get a sandwich, and THEN I remembered I needed to pick up my prescription at the pharmacy, and by the time I got out of there it was too late.

jokes.text: George points out to Greg that he saw someone urinating in the shower — without yet knowing Greg is Elaine's crush
jokes.setup: George is at the health club where he committed the shower offense; he doesn't know Greg is connected to Elaine
jokes.punchline: George is essentially self-narrating his own undoing — telling the witness about the incident he's trying to cover up, compounding his predicament
jokes.explanation: Plot escalation — the storylines are converging. Comedy comes from dramatic irony: we know Greg is who George is talking to. Functional but not standalone funny.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You're married?!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, Uncle Leo, I got married.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: When did this happen? Why didn't anybody tell me?

jokes.text: Jerry asks Meryl to get him a drink from the other room. She's right there. 'You're right there.' / 'Come on, I'm sitting.'
jokes.setup: Jerry calls for Meryl to get him something to drink
jokes.punchline: She's sitting right next to him — the request is maximally lazy, and Meryl's protest is perfectly reasonable, but Jerry invokes 'I'm sitting' as full justification
jokes.explanation: Good domestic comedy — the fake marriage immediately producing genuine spousal laziness. 'I'm sitting' as complete justification is a great economy punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You're getting married and you didn't tell me? Your own uncle!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Uncle Leo, I was going to tell you.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: When? At the wedding? I have to hear it from Cousin Jeffrey? I'm family, Jerry. Your blood!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Uncle Leo, come on.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: No, don't 'come on' me. I'm hurt. I'm very hurt. Do you know what this does to me?

jokes.text: The can opener argument: Jerry insists it's in the drawer, Meryl says it isn't, neither will look properly; escalates to 'Well, wouldn't I have to be stupid?'
jokes.setup: A mundane argument about a missing can opener
jokes.punchline: The argument escalates from a factual dispute to an implied insult about intelligence — classic domestic-fight logic where object location becomes a referendum on someone's competence
jokes.explanation: Well-observed domestic argument escalation. 'What other conclusion could one reach?' is a great line — George-level rationalization applied to a can opener. The argument follows the authentic logic of real couples' fights.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: I'm a good uncle! I give gifts, I call, I show interest in your life.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Uncle Leo, you're a terrible uncle.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Remember that letter you wrote me? The one with all the V's in it? You formed those V's beautifully. I showed it to everyone at the coffee shop.

jokes.text: Jerry's parents call — they've heard Jerry got married from Uncle Leo — while Jerry is simultaneously mid-can-opener fight with Meryl
jokes.setup: Jerry is fighting with Meryl over the can opener
jokes.punchline: His parents call simultaneously about the 'marriage,' creating overlapping conversations where the domestic argument bleeds into the family call — Jerry managing both while Meryl is audibly fighting in the background
jokes.explanation: Two plotlines converge at once: Leo's gossip reaches the parents, and the parents call during a real domestic argument. The chaos of simultaneous conversations (parents asking about marriage while Meryl fights in background) is great structural comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Can you pick up some olives for me?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Olives? You're asking me to pick up olives? That's so romantic!

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: It's just olives.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No one has ever asked me to get olives before. This means something.

jokes.text: 'I'm just pretending I'm married to get a discount on dry cleaning.' (Jerry explains to his parents)
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents are thrilled about the marriage; his mother was apparently proven wrong about whether Jerry would ever marry
jokes.punchline: Jerry flatly explains the actual reason — a discount on dry cleaning — which is so anticlimactic it perfectly punctures the family's excitement
jokes.explanation: The ultimate deflation of the marriage premise delivered to the most invested audience (his parents). 'A discount on dry cleaning' as the explanation for an apparent marriage is a perfect economy joke — the absurdity is in the gap between the emotional weight and the actual cause.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: You look amazing today.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh please, I look like I've been dragged through a hedge backwards. Now me yesterday? That was something special.

jokes.text: 'Could you make a little more noise?' / pause / 'Well, whoopee-woo.'
jokes.setup: The can opener has apparently been found (or the argument is ending)
jokes.punchline: 'Whoopee-woo' as Meryl's (or Jerry's) deflated celebration of finding the can opener is perfectly anti-climactic
jokes.explanation: Functional beat ending the can opener sequence. 'Whoopee-woo' is an amusingly deflated victory celebration. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I had this dream last night.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What was it about?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Well, I was at this party, and suddenly everyone turned into pretzels.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Pretzels? That's the dumbest thing I've ever heard. You know what? I'm breaking up with you.

jokes.text: Meryl can't sleep without her quilt: 'The other night I was cold, so last night I turned up the heat, it's too hot. I opened up a window, it's too cold. I can't get into the zone.'
jokes.setup: Meryl is exhausted because her quilt is at the cleaners
jokes.punchline: 'I can't get into the zone' — a sports/self-help phrase applied to achieving optimal sleep temperature — is wonderfully specific and recognizable
jokes.explanation: Good observational joke about temperature calibration for sleep. 'Get into the zone' applied to sleeping comfort is funny in its earnestness. Recognizable domestic frustration well-expressed.

jokes.text: Jerry brings out a secret quilt he's been hiding — he forgot it was there
jokes.setup: Meryl has been suffering without her quilt while it's at the cleaners
jokes.punchline: Jerry has had a quilt right there the whole time and forgot — deflating Meryl's suffering entirely
jokes.explanation: Competent situational irony — the suffering was unnecessary. Not a particularly strong joke on its own but good plot mechanics.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I saw George with his hand in the cookie jar.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? You're going to report him?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: You bet I am. He's going down.

jokes.text: The fake-marriage couple has a genuine 'making up after a fight' moment — 'I love you' / 'I love you' — complete sincerity despite the whole relationship being fake
jokes.setup: Jerry and Meryl have been arguing like a real married couple
jokes.punchline: They now make up like a real married couple — the fake marriage has somehow become emotionally real, which is both sweet and absurd
jokes.explanation: The irony of the fake marriage becoming behaviorally real is the thematic heart of the episode. The moment earns its warmth while remaining fundamentally absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, could you get me a drink?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why don't you get it yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm comfortable.

jokes.text: I'm sorry about all that can opener stuff. / Yeah. Me too. / I love you. / I love you. / Well, good night. / Good night.
jokes.setup: Jerry and Meryl resolve their can opener fight.
jokes.punchline: The couple's reconciliation — 'I love you / I love you / Good night' — performed with total sincerity, despite being a fake couple. The fake marriage has produced a real marriage ritual.
jokes.explanation: A lovely quiet joke. The entire arc of a couple fight and reconciliation completed in a pretend marriage, with genuine 'I love you' exchange. The comedy is in the sincerity of fake intimacy — they've both bought into the bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Where's the can opener?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't know, where did you last have it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: If I knew that, I wouldn't be asking you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well maybe if you actually kept track of things instead of just leaving them everywhere...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, so now it's my fault? You're the one who borrowed it last.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I borrowed it? I never borrowed your can opener!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes you did. Three weeks ago. For the beans.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Those weren't my beans, Jerry. Those were your beans!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They were in your apartment!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Because you left them there!

jokes.text: Elaine re-analyzes: 'He even wiped his hand on the top of the bottle when I offered him water.' / 'That doesn't mean anything.' / 'Are you kidding? That's very significant. If he was interested in me, he'd want my germs. He'd just crave my germs.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is now reading the bottle-wipe as a negative signal
jokes.punchline: 'He'd just crave my germs' — romantic interest expressed as pathogen desire — is a brilliantly disgusting and absurdist formulation
jokes.explanation: 'He'd just crave my germs' is one of the episode's best lines. The idea that romantic attraction manifests as a desire to share pathogens is both disgusting and weirdly logical — it has the feel of a genuine insight delivered in the wrongest possible terms. Highly quotable, original, and perfectly Elaine. Earns 8.5 for the phrase alone.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not stupid, I just made a mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, if you weren't stupid, you wouldn't have made the mistake. So logically, you are stupid.

jokes.text: 'Bottle-wipe supersedes it.' (Jerry, on bottle-wipe overruling the open-lip kiss as a signal)
jokes.setup: The group is debating the hierarchy of romantic signals: open-lip kiss vs. bottle-wipe
jokes.punchline: Jerry delivers the definitive ruling — 'bottle-wipe supersedes it' — with legal/judicial authority on a completely absurd taxonomy of romantic gestures
jokes.explanation: Three words that land like a judicial verdict on a completely fabricated social code. The economy is near-perfect: 'bottle-wipe supersedes it' treats an absurd romantic signal hierarchy with the gravity of contract law. One of the episode's most quotable moments. Strong economy and structure carry this to 8.5.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I told her we should get married.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You're getting married?!

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Jerry, that's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: When's the wedding? We have to tell everyone!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa - she hasn't even said yes yet.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: She'll say yes! Of course she'll say yes!

jokes.text: 'Could it be because you don't want him to know that your friend pees in the shower?'
jokes.setup: Elaine explains she can't ask Greg out now (after the bottle-wipe rejection theory)
jokes.punchline: George cuts to the real reason Elaine is reluctant — his shower incident has made her radioactive by association
jokes.explanation: Good convergence joke — George's shower incident is now affecting Elaine's love life. The blunt phrasing 'your friend pees in the shower' is a strong economy punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have something to tell you. I got married.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You're married?!

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: To who?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not actually married. I just told people I was married to get a discount on a car rental.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You lied about being married for a car rental discount?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was a really good discount!

jokes.text: 'I was there. I saw a drain.' / 'Since when is a drain a toilet?' / 'It's all pipes.' / 'What's the difference?' / 'Different pipes go to different places.' / 'You're gonna mix them up.'
jokes.setup: George is defending his shower urination with plumbing logic
jokes.punchline: George's position that 'it's all pipes' and the pipes just go to different places — treating toilet plumbing as interchangeable with shower plumbing — is a perfectly George rationalization: technically defensible by a moron, immediately dismantled
jokes.explanation: Superb George character comedy. 'I saw a drain' and 'it's all pipes' are perfectly George — confident, self-serving logic that is just wrong enough to be absurd but just plausible enough to take a second to dismantle. This is the George Costanza signature move: bulletproof confidence in an indefensible position. Earns 8.5 for character integration and the specific phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, you're giving up? Well, that's great! That's just wonderful! I'm so proud of you right now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you want me to do?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I want you to try! I want you to care! But no, it's easier just to give up, isn't it?

jokes.text: 'Can we just drop the pee-pipes stuff?'
jokes.setup: The debate about shower plumbing has gotten out of hand
jokes.punchline: Elaine names the discussion 'pee-pipes stuff' and demands it end — the phrase 'pee-pipes' is both accurate and hilarious
jokes.explanation: Two words — 'pee-pipes stuff' — perfectly name and dismiss the entire argument. The coinage is funny and immediately quotable. Elaine's frustrated pragmatism is well-characterized.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I can't sleep, Jerry. I've been up for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Three days? What happened to your quilt?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's the thing - I don't have it anymore. And without it, I can't fall asleep. I lie there, I toss and turn, my mind races. I think about everything - my childhood, my regrets, that time I wore a bro. And then I get hungry, so I go to the kitchen, but there's nothing good to eat. So I stand there in the dark, staring into the refrigerator like it's going to tell me the meaning of life. And by the time I close the door, I've forgotten why I opened it in the first place.

jokes.text: 'It has to be ready. What kind of a business are you running here?' / 'Who the hell are you? It's not your quilt.' / 'He's a good friend of mine. He's like an older brother to me. When things don't go right, he takes it personally.'
jokes.setup: The quilt isn't ready at the dry cleaner — Meryl/Jerry pushes back
jokes.punchline: The dry cleaner correctly identifies that this isn't Jerry's quilt (it's Meryl's) — and Meryl/Jerry explains their relationship with a hilariously non-specific defense ('like an older brother to me')
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy at the dry cleaner. The 'older brother' explanation is mildly amusing but the joke doesn't have a sharp punchline — it's more situational texture than a real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I love you too.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, this is weird.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, we never say that.

jokes.text: I can't believe you still do that. It's bad for you. / That's how I maintain my glow.
jokes.setup: George mentions he's going to use tanning machines despite looking terrible.
jokes.punchline: 'That's how I maintain my glow' — George's vain self-description of tanning bed usage as 'maintaining his glow,' despite having just been told he looks pale and terrible.
jokes.explanation: 'My glow' is a funny self-designation for George — a character not known for radiant attractiveness. The confidence with which he says it while looking pale is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So if he wipes the bottle, he's not interested. If he doesn't wipe the bottle, he likes you?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not insane. It means he wants your germs. He's attracted to you.

jokes.text: A person asks how much it costs to clean an item — $13 — and says 'I can't afford that' and leaves
jokes.setup: A bystander inquires about cleaning costs
jokes.punchline: The quiet dignity of 'I can't afford that' and leaving — a tiny, perfectly observed human moment
jokes.explanation: A small, dry observational beat. Works as ambient New York comedy texture — the kind of incidental character Seinfeld is known for — but it's not a strong standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that wiping off a bottle is a bigger sign of affection than kissing?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Absolutely. If a woman wipes off a bottle for you, she's thinking about your welfare. Kissing could mean anything.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's insane. Wiping off a bottle is hygiene, not romance.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But George has a point. She's protecting you from germs. That's love.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! It's the small gestures that matter.

jokes.text: Greg leaves sweat on the tanning machine without wiping it — Elaine interprets it as a signal of intimacy: 'He knew I'd use it next. He didn't wipe his sweat off. That's a gesture of intimacy.'
jokes.setup: Elaine was devastated by the bottle-wipe; now she's re-analyzing
jokes.punchline: Leaving sweat on a machine — the opposite of bottle-wiping — is now read as a positive romantic signal. The signal taxonomy is self-contradicting and entirely Elaine's invention
jokes.explanation: The sweat signal perfectly mirrors and extends the germ-craving logic from earlier. Leaving sweat is intimate; wiping is rejection. Elaine's romantic semiotics are now a complete, internally consistent but objectively insane framework. Strong character work and a great callback.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You just want to see him because he's unavailable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That is not true!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Okay, maybe a little.

jokes.text: 'A guy leaves sweat, that's a signal?' / 'Yes. It's a social thing.' / 'If he left a used Kleenex, what's that, a valentine?'
jokes.setup: George challenges whether sweat-leaving is a real signal
jokes.punchline: Jerry escalates to the logical extreme — a used Kleenex as the ultimate romantic token — 'what's that, a valentine?' The absurdist escalation is perfectly calibrated
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. 'If he left a used Kleenex, what's that, a valentine?' is a perfect escalation of the bodily-fluid-as-romance framework. The word 'valentine' in this context is inspired — it's simultaneously the most romantic and most revolting image possible. Great economy, perfect structure, genuinely original. Earns its high score alongside joke #34 as the episode's co-best moments.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't just mix the pipes like that!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not? They're all pipes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not how plumbing works, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, a pipe is a pipe. Hot water, cold water—it's all the same to the pipe.

jokes.text: 'So you're gonna let me get suspended for shower urination?'
jokes.setup: Jerry refuses to intervene with Greg on George's behalf
jokes.punchline: 'Shower urination' as a formal charge — 'suspended for shower urination' — treats the act with the gravity of a professional misconduct allegation
jokes.explanation: The formal language 'suspended for shower urination' is a brilliant George line — he's treating his gross misconduct as a legitimate grievance requiring due process. The institutional framing of a bathroom act is perfectly absurd. Strong economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You want professional confirmation? I'll get you professional confirmation. I know a guy.

jokes.text: If he left a used Kleenex, what's that, a valentine?
jokes.setup: Elaine argues that not wiping sweat off a machine is a romantic signal.
jokes.punchline: George extends the logic to its absurd extreme: by that reasoning, a used Kleenex would be a valentine.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. George reduces Elaine's signal theory to its logical absurdity in one perfectly economical question. 'A used Kleenex, what's that, a valentine?' is brilliantly structured: the specific grossness of a used Kleenex + the romantic register of 'valentine' creates a perfect comedic collision. The question format is better than any declarative version would be. Highly quotable, near-perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You look a little pale.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Pale? I can't look pale! I'm meeting her parents!

jokes.text: 'I can't let you do this.' / 'I want to.' / 'But it isn't right. I can't.' / 'Give me the clothes.' / 'Jerry, please.' / 'What about her?' / 'Oh, the hell with her.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is conflicted about bringing Meryl's (or another woman's) dry cleaning in
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, the hell with her' — Meryl dismisses the other woman (the real beneficiary of the discount situation) with breezy callousness, revealing the fake wife has developed real wife territorial instincts
jokes.explanation: 'The hell with her' as the fake wife's territorial response is a good character beat. The melodrama of the exchange ('I can't let you do this / I want to') is deliberately overwrought for a dry cleaning errand, which is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My quilt has to be ready by Friday. It's a quilt, for crying out loud. How long can it possibly take?

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: Sir, a quilt is not a simple garment. It requires special handling, careful cleaning—

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Special handling? It's a quilt! My grandmother made it in an afternoon!

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: When do you need it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Friday!

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: That is two days away.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly! Two days! How is that not enough time?

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: Why are you in such a rush with a quilt?

jokes.text: 'No, Jerry, please. I'm not gonna let you walk out of my life.' (about picking up dry cleaning)
jokes.setup: The dry cleaning exchange has become melodramatic
jokes.punchline: 'I'm not gonna let you walk out of my life' is a soap opera breakup line delivered about a laundry errand — the romantic register is completely wrong for the stakes
jokes.explanation: The melodrama-vs.-mundanity gap is the joke engine here. Using 'I'm not gonna let you walk out of my life' for a dry cleaning pickup is a solid ironic deployment. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you see, when someone does something inconsiderate, even something small, it's like they're declaring war on civility itself. So naturally, I have to respond with proportional aggression to maintain the social contract.

jokes.text: 'Do you want box or hanger?' / 'You decide.' (exchanged with romantic gravity)
jokes.setup: The scene has been melodramatic relationship territory
jokes.punchline: The conflict resolves on 'box or hanger' — the ultimate anticlimax for what seemed like an emotional watershed. Meryl's 'You decide' even has a reconciliation tone
jokes.explanation: Superb deflation. The emotional buildup of the 'I'm not gonna let you walk out of my life' scene punctured by 'Box or hanger?' is textbook Seinfeld anticlimax. 'You decide' — delivered like a romantic concession — is perfect. Excellent economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Jerry, I'm telling you, I gotta maintain the glow. You know what I'm saying? The glow is everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, you're gonna kill yourself with those tanning beds.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't care! You think Fabio worries about skin cancer? No! He's got the glow, and that's all that matters.

jokes.text: 'You know, I'm engaged.' / 'Yep, I'm getting married in 50 years.'
jokes.setup: Elaine mentions her engagement to Greg at the gym
jokes.punchline: She's referencing the joke-pact with Jerry — she's technically 'engaged' (to Jerry, in 50 years) — the absurdity of citing this as a real engagement is perfectly delivered
jokes.explanation: Good callback. '50 years' returns as a punchline. The deadpan delivery of this as a genuine engagement is consistent with Elaine's matter-of-fact absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: How much is it to clean this shirt?

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: Five dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Five dollars?! That's outrageous! I can't afford that!

jokes.text: You know, I'm engaged. / Yep, I'm getting married in 50 years.
jokes.setup: Elaine mentions to George that she's engaged.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm getting married in 50 years' — the 10-years-become-50-years 'engagement' is delivered with straight-faced acceptance of the absurd terms.
jokes.explanation: Good callback. Elaine casually mentions the engagement to George as if it's real, and the specific '50 years' detail makes it funnier than a vaguer reference. The deadpan delivery of a completely absurd timeline is very Seinfeld.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm next on the tanning bed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I've been waiting for twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Oh, you want it? It's all yours.

jokes.text: 'Correct me if I'm wrong, but isn't it a violation of club policy to not wipe down a machine after using it?' (Elaine to Greg, threatening him with the sweat rule)
jokes.setup: Greg was about to report George for shower urination; Elaine counters
jokes.punchline: Elaine weaponizes the sweat-signal she just interpreted as romantic intimacy — the thing she loved is now a violation she's using as blackmail
jokes.explanation: Brilliant structural callback: the sweat she interpreted as romantic intimacy becomes her blackmail weapon. The pivot from 'gesture of intimacy' to 'violation of club policy' on the SAME ACT is great writing. Strong character integration and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what did Greg say?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Nothing! He just stood there. He didn't even wipe the sweat off his forehead.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So? That's romantic! He's not wiping the sweat. He wants me to see him sweat. He's confident!

jokes.text: Well, correct me if I'm wrong, but isn't it a violation of club policy to not wipe down a machine after using it?
jokes.setup: Greg is about to report George for shower urination; Elaine needs to stop him.
jokes.punchline: Elaine weaponizes the sweat signal against Greg — using his own club-violation as leverage, with beautifully polite framing ('correct me if I'm wrong').
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the sweat signal subplot. The callback works because Elaine uses the very evidence they debated as a 'signal' vs. 'violation' as actual leverage. 'Correct me if I'm wrong' is a perfect polite framing for blackmail. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You can't just call someone back after you've already hung up on them. That's a rule.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, what if I call Greg back right now and hang up on him again? Then we're even.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I see. You're friends with the urinator, aren't you?' / 'Yeah, well... at least he had a drain.'
jokes.setup: Greg realizes Elaine is defending George
jokes.punchline: 'At least he had a drain' — Elaine's defense of George is the weakest possible defense, conceding the act while noting he was near appropriate plumbing
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a perfect echo of George's own 'it's all pipes' / 'I saw a drain' defense. 'At least he had a drain' is the worst possible character reference — it's technically true and completely damning at the same time. Perfect economy (five words), great callback, highly quotable. Earns its 8.6+ for the devastatingly funny specificity.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Sweat is a sign of passion! It means I care!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, that's beautiful, George. You know what? I'm going to give you a used Kleenex for Valentine's Day.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A used Kleenex?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, it shows I care. It's got my DNA all over it.

jokes.text: There you go, Mrs. Seinfeld. With your 25 percent discount, it comes to $17.80.
jokes.setup: The entire fake-wife discount scheme running through the episode.
jokes.punchline: The dry cleaner addresses Meryl as 'Mrs. Seinfeld' — fully buying the fake marriage — and the 25% discount results in a still-significant bill of $17.80.
jokes.explanation: A good running-gag beat. 'Mrs. Seinfeld' is funny, and the specific dollar amount ($17.80 after 25% off) adds a mundane precision. The scheme is paying off — literally — which is good structural comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You gotta threaten him. Tell him you'll report him for shower urination.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Shower urination?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, he'll get suspended for shower urination.

jokes.text: Meryl picks up Jerry's dry cleaning as 'Mrs. Seinfeld' — and receives a stranger's clothes that Jerry brought in
jokes.setup: Jerry has been secretly using the fake-wife discount for someone else's dry cleaning
jokes.punchline: Meryl receives clothing that isn't hers — the fake marriage is now producing fake-wife infidelity with another woman's wardrobe
jokes.explanation: Good structural escalation — the fake marriage's logic is now producing marital betrayal. The reveal that Jerry was using the discount for another woman's clothes mirrors real infidelity within the fake-marriage framework.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Could you give me a discount? I'm a little short on cash.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Excuse me, this isn't mine. / Oh, yes, it is. Your husband brought it in himself. / Really? / Thank you.
jokes.setup: Meryl is given something she doesn't recognize as hers.
jokes.punchline: Jerry has secretly dropped off Meryl's clothing WITHOUT telling her — playing the devoted fake-husband role behind her back — and she receives it with pleased surprise.
jokes.explanation: A nice twist. The discovery that Jerry has been secretly doing fake-husband duties creates a warm-funny moment — he's been more committed to the fake marriage than he admitted. Rewatch bonus for the implications about Jerry's actual feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How could you do this to me? After everything we've been through!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, I know. I never meant to hurt you.

dialogue.speaker: Dry cleaner
dialogue.line: Excuse me, do you want starch in the shirt or no starch?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Medium starch.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Medium starch?! That's what you're worried about right now?

jokes.text: 'You son of a bitch. Who is she? I want to know who she is.' / 'It doesn't matter.' / 'I want a divorce.'
jokes.setup: Meryl has discovered Jerry used the discount for another woman
jokes.punchline: Meryl demands a divorce from a fake marriage — treating the fake marital betrayal with full emotional weight of a real one
jokes.explanation: A great payoff — the fake marriage produces a real breakup. 'I want a divorce' from a relationship that was never real is the episode's thematic punchline. The emotional authenticity of the scene is what makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're not going to say anything to her, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Well, I have to report it. It's the right thing to do.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, but she's really attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I'm reporting it.

jokes.text: 'A divorce? So you can marry her and give her the discount?'
jokes.setup: Meryl demands a divorce
jokes.punchline: Her jealousy isn't about love — it's about losing the dry cleaning discount to a rival 'wife.' The marital grievance is entirely mercenary
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes and a perfect thematic summary. The divorce is not about love but about losing the discount — the fake marriage has been entirely transactional from the start, and this line makes that explicit in the most devastating way. Great economy and structure. Meryl's wounded 'so you can marry her and give HER the discount?' treats 25% off dry cleaning as the ultimate marital prize.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I've been engaged for 50 years.

jokes.text: "I want a divorce." / "A divorce? So you can marry her and give her the discount?" / "Yes, that's right."
jokes.setup: Meryl demands a divorce
jokes.punchline: The 'divorce' is motivated entirely by wanting to deny the next woman the discount — that's the real betrayal. And Jerry confirms it.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's crown jewels. The whole fake marriage premise crystallizes into its purest comic essence: she's not jealous of the other woman per se — she's jealous of her getting the discount. And Jerry's 'Yes, that's right' confirms that the dry cleaning benefit was always the point. The absurdist logic is airtight and hilarious. Rivals the caliber of the best Seinfeld moments.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I'm going to report George for violating company policy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh really? Well, maybe I should report you for that time you were looking at adult websites on the company computer.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: That was... that was different.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Different how? A violation is a violation.

jokes.text: 'What happened to us, Jerry?' / 'I'll tell you what happened. We got married.' / 'I'm sorry. This is my fault. I pushed it on you.' / 'No. I guess I just wasn't ready for the responsibilities of a pretend marriage.'
jokes.setup: The fake marriage is 'ending' in a breakup scene
jokes.punchline: 'I just wasn't ready for the responsibilities of a pretend marriage' — the acknowledgment that the fake marriage had real responsibilities, and that Jerry failed to meet even those, is a perfect absurdist-ironic closer
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic punchline.
