'I wasn't ready for the responsibilities of a pretend marriage' is the distilled essence of what makes this episode great — it acknowledges that the fake marriage became real enough to require responsibility, and that Jerry couldn't even manage the pretend version. Beautifully constructed, highly quotable, and earns 9+ for being a genuinely original formulation that works as both comedy and character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... you're the same person who took a shower in my apartment!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, at least that shower had a drain.

jokes.text: Yes, that's right. / What happened to us, Jerry? / I'll tell you what happened. We got married.
jokes.setup: Meryl asks what happened to 'us' — their fake marriage relationship.
jokes.punchline: 'We got married.' Jerry's diagnosis: the act of getting married (even fake) is what destroyed the relationship. Marriage itself is the problem.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a genuine Seinfeld thesis statement. 'We got married' as the answer to 'what happened to us?' has both comic economy and real philosophical weight. The joke works because it's true within the episode (the fake marriage produced real couple problems) AND it's a broader comment on marriage. Perfect structure — the setup question ('what happened to us?') is a romantic cliché, the answer ('we got married') is both the literal truth and the absurd punchline. Near the 9.5 anchor in quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: These are not your wife's clothes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean? Of course they are.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: No, no. These belong to another woman.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's impossible. My wife brought them in.

dialogue.speaker: Dry Cleaner
dialogue.line: Your wife? This is not your wife's size. This is much smaller.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, maybe she's on a diet.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. This is my fault. I pushed it on you. / No. I guess I just wasn't ready for the responsibilities of a pretend marriage.
jokes.setup: The fake-couple is breaking up after their fake-marriage drama.
jokes.punchline: 'I guess I just wasn't ready for the responsibilities of a pretend marriage.' Jerry applies the language of genuine relationship failure to a fictional arrangement.
jokes.explanation: 'The responsibilities of a pretend marriage' is one of the best lines of the episode. The phrase is perfectly constructed — 'pretend marriage' as a thing that has 'responsibilities' one might not be ready for is simultaneously absurd and, within the episode's logic, completely true. The deadpan sincerity with which Jerry delivers it makes it both funny and oddly poignant. The joke is the episode's thesis: even fake marriages are too much commitment for Jerry. Approaches the highest-tier anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, you're having an affair?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, it's not like that—

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's it. I want a divorce.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, we're not even married!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, we are now. And now we're getting divorced.

jokes.text: 'We'll always have pancakes.' (Meryl's goodbye, echoing Casablanca)
jokes.setup: 'We'll always have Paris' from Casablanca — the classic romantic farewell
jokes.punchline: 'Pancakes' — their shared romantic touchstone is the breakfast they almost ordered matching
jokes.explanation: A lovely callback that references Casablanca while deflating it perfectly. 'We'll always have pancakes' as the romantic keepsake of this fake marriage — pointing back to their first scene together — is both funny and genuinely touching. Well-constructed, quotable, good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're only getting divorced because of the money, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, yeah. What else would I get divorced for?

jokes.text: 'I thought you said you was bringing a white boy home. I don't see a white boy. I see a damn fool.' (Anna's grandmother reacting to George after the tanning)
jokes.setup: George has been using tanning machines to look healthy for meeting Anna's parents — presumably overdone
jokes.punchline: The grandmother's assessment cuts through George's vanity with brutal, direct honesty — 'I see a damn fool' is both about his appearance and his general character
jokes.explanation: Strong cold-ending joke. The grandmother's delivery is perfectly timed — the setup ('white boy') is unexpected, and 'I see a damn fool' is a great punchline that works on multiple levels (the tanning, George's overall foolishness). Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what are we doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, that's the thing about marriage. It's not a relationship, it's a sentence.

jokes.text: I thought you said you was bringing a white boy home. I don't see a white boy. I see a damn fool.
jokes.setup: George has arrived to meet Anna's parents, apparently tanned from the tanning machine.
jokes.punchline: Anna's father expected a white boy; George's tanning has made him unrecognizable as such. 'I see a damn fool' is the verdict on both his appearance and his choices.
jokes.explanation: A strong payoff joke to the George tanning subplot. 'I see a damn fool' works as both a description of his overcooked appearance and a character judgment. The joke earns its laugh because the setup has been building — we knew George was going to look ridiculous meeting Anna's parents after the tanning machine. The father's directness is the perfect punchline delivery mechanism.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's my fault. I take full responsibility for this marriage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I have to tell you something. I've been seeing someone else.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, every Thursday night I go to the movies with my mother. I know it's wrong, but I just can't help myself.

jokes.text: Stand-up: 'To me, the thing about marriage is I can't believe how often it happens... I can't believe that many people are meeting people that they want to see every single day, every day, every day.'
jokes.setup: Jerry reflects on how common marriage is
jokes.punchline: The sheer frequency of people wanting to see the same person every day strikes Jerry as statistically improbable — 'that should happen like three or four times in the whole century'
jokes.explanation: Solid stand-up observation that thematically ties to the episode. The 'every day, every day, every day' repetition is effective. 'Three or four times in the whole century' is a nicely absurd quantification. Good closing stand-up but not as sharp as the dry-clean only bit.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, I'm returning your syrup.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My syrup? That's my syrup.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, I borrowed it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, in divorce proceedings, the judge would consider this a very generous gesture.

jokes.text: To me, the thing about marriage is I can't believe how often it happens. I like the idea of it, but I can't believe that many people are meeting people that they want to see every single day, every day, every day, every day. That should happen, like, three or four times in the whole century.
jokes.setup: Closing stand-up on marriage frequency.
jokes.punchline: Marriage should happen 'three or four times in the whole century' — framing the prevalence of marriage as statistically improbable and cosmically unlikely.
jokes.explanation: A solid stand-up observation that connects thematically to the episode. The 'every day every day every day' repetition effectively dramatizes the terror of the commitment. 'Three or four times in the whole century' is a good hyperbolic reframe. Competent stand-up that echoes the episode's themes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We'll always have the Upper West Side.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: We'll always have Monk's.

jokes.text: Stand-up: The marriage-as-car-purchase extended metaphor: ring=keys, 'start him up,' 'value drops 20 percent when you drive each other off the lot,' 'till death do us part'=extended warranty
jokes.setup: Jerry extends marriage to a consumer purchase metaphor
jokes.punchline: Multiple nested comparisons: the ring as car keys, driving off the lot as value depreciation, the extended warranty as the death vow — each element of the car-buying process maps onto marriage with escalating absurdity
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed extended metaphor. The 'extended warranty' for 'till death do us part' is particularly clever. 'Value drops 20 percent when you drive each other off the lot' is a good line. Solid professional stand-up with a clever structure.

dialogue.speaker: Anna's parent
dialogue.line: What happened to you?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm tan.

dialogue.speaker: Anna's parent
dialogue.line: You look like a chestnut.

tmdb_overview: Jerry's girlfriend poses as his wife to get in on his dry-cleaning discount.
insight: High-density character comedy sustains 89.6 score across 93 jokes in 21 minutes.
